title: 1835 Constitution of Mexico

infobox.value: was a [constitution](/wiki/List_of_constitutions_of_Mexico "List of constitutions of Mexico") that fundamentally altered the organizational structure of [Mexico](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico"), away from the federal structure established by the [Constitution of 1824](/wiki/1824_Constitution_of_Mexico "1824 Constitution of Mexico"), thus ending the [First Mexican Republic](/wiki/First_Mexican_Republic "First Mexican Republic") and creating a [unitary republic](/wiki/Unitary_republic "Unitary republic"), the [Centralist Republic of Mexico](/wiki/Centralist_Republic_of_Mexico "Centralist Republic of Mexico").[[1]](#cite_note-1) Formalized under [President](/wiki/Mexican_President "Mexican President") [Antonio López de Santa Anna](/wiki/Antonio_L%C3%B3pez_de_Santa_Anna "Antonio López de Santa Anna") on 15 December 1835, they were enacted in 1836. The Seven Laws curtailed the autonomy of states, turning them into mere departments with governors appointed by the president.[[2]](#cite_note-”Schenoni2024”-2) They were intended to centralize and strengthen the national government. The aim of the previous constitution was to create a political system that would emulate the success of the United States, but after a decade of political turmoil, economic stagnation, and threats and actual foreign invasion, conservatives concluded that a better path for Mexico was centralized power.
Felipe Tena Ramírez, *Leyes fundamentales de México, 1808-1971*. pp. 202-248.

Schenoni, Luis L. (2024). [*Bringing War Back In: Victory, Defeat, and the State in Nineteenth-Century Latin America*](https://www.cambridge.org/core/books/bringing-war-back-in/849FF87CA1DC04EDE8657C90F00D9504). Cambridge University Press. p. 227. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [9781009442145](/wiki/Special:BookSources/9781009442145 "Special:BookSources/9781009442145").

Michael P. Costeloe, "Siete Leyes (1836)" in *[Encyclopedia of Latin American History and Culture](/wiki/Encyclopedia_of_Latin_American_History_and_Culture "Encyclopedia of Latin American History and Culture")*, vol. 4, p. 25. New York: Charles Scribner's Sons 1996.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [1835 Constitution of Mexico](/w/index.php?title=1835_Constitution_of_Mexico&redirect=no "1835 Constitution of Mexico"))

Mexican constitutional changes of 1835–1836

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2a/Siete_Leyes_Illustrated.png/330px-Siete_Leyes_Illustrated.png)](/wiki/File:Siete_Leyes_Illustrated.png)

Diagram illustrating the government organized by the Siete Leyes

***Las Siete Leyes*** (Spanish: [[las ˈsjete ˈleʝes]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Spanish "Help:IPA/Spanish"), or **Seven Laws** was a [constitution](/wiki/List_of_constitutions_of_Mexico "List of constitutions of Mexico") that fundamentally altered the organizational structure of [Mexico](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico"), away from the federal structure established by the [Constitution of 1824](/wiki/1824_Constitution_of_Mexico "1824 Constitution of Mexico"), thus ending the [First Mexican Republic](/wiki/First_Mexican_Republic "First Mexican Republic") and creating a [unitary republic](/wiki/Unitary_republic "Unitary republic"), the [Centralist Republic of Mexico](/wiki/Centralist_Republic_of_Mexico "Centralist Republic of Mexico").[[1]](#cite_note-1) Formalized under [President](/wiki/Mexican_President "Mexican President") [Antonio López de Santa Anna](/wiki/Antonio_L%C3%B3pez_de_Santa_Anna "Antonio López de Santa Anna") on 15 December 1835, they were enacted in 1836. The Seven Laws curtailed the autonomy of states, turning them into mere departments with governors appointed by the president.[[2]](#cite_note-”Schenoni2024”-2) They were intended to centralize and strengthen the national government. The aim of the previous constitution was to create a political system that would emulate the success of the United States, but after a decade of political turmoil, economic stagnation, and threats and actual foreign invasion, conservatives concluded that a better path for Mexico was centralized power.

1. The 15 articles of the first law granted [citizenship](/wiki/Citizenship "Citizenship") to those who could read Spanish and had an annual income of 100 [pesos](/wiki/Mexican_peso "Mexican peso"), except for male [domestic workers](/wiki/Domestic_worker "Domestic worker"), who did not have the right to vote, nor did women of any class.
2. The second law allowed the President to close [Congress](/wiki/Congress_of_Mexico "Congress of Mexico") and suppress the [Supreme Court of Justice of the Nation](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_Mexico "Supreme Court of Mexico"). Military officers were not allowed to assume this office.
3. The 58 articles of the third law established a [bicameral](/wiki/Bicameral "Bicameral") Congress of Deputies and Senators, elected by governmental organs. Deputies had four-year terms; Senators were elected for six years.
4. The 34 articles of the fourth law specified that the Supreme Court, the [Senate of Mexico](/wiki/Senate_of_Mexico "Senate of Mexico"), and the Meeting of [Ministers](/wiki/Political_minister "Political minister") each nominate three candidates, and the lower house of the legislature would select from those nine candidates the President and [Vice-president](/wiki/Vice-president "Vice-president"),
5. The fifth law had an 11-member Supreme Court elected in the same manner as the President and vice-president.
6. The 31 articles of the sixth Law replaced the federal republic's nominally-[sovereign](/wiki/Sovereignty "Sovereignty") "[states](/wiki/States_of_Mexico "States of Mexico")" with centralized "[departments](/wiki/Department_(country_subdivision) "Department (country subdivision)")", fashioned after the [French model](/wiki/Departments_of_France "Departments of France"), whose governors and legislators were designated by the President.
7. The seventh law prohibited reverting to the pre-reform laws for six years.

*Las Siete Leyes* were replaced in 1843 by the *[Bases Orgánicas](/wiki/Bases_Org%C3%A1nicas "Bases Orgánicas")*.[[3]](#cite_note-3)
